explanation: The requirement states that prop_2 should occur once both prop_0 and prop_1 have occurred. In LTL, this can be captured by stating that until both prop_0 and prop_1 have occurred, prop_2 does not happen, and eventually prop_2 does happen. Once prop_2 happens, it is not required again.
This formula expresses that prop_0 must happen at some point after both prop_1 and prop_2 have occurred, and that prop_0 does not happen before both prop_1 and prop_2 have happened.

explanation: The system should transition from Start-up Mode to Self test Mode as soon as both prop_1 and prop_2 become true. Using only atomic propositions and LTL operators, and assuming 'start' represents being in Start-up Mode and 'self_test' represents being in Self test Mode, the transition is: if in Start-up Mode, eventually when both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, this triggers a transition to Self test Mode in the next state. In LTL, the statement reads: whenever 'start' holds, once prop_1 and prop_2 hold, then in the next step 'self_test' holds and not 'start' anymore. Since only the given atomic propositions are allowed, and if mode variables are not atomic propositions, we may only express that 'eventually (prop_1 & prop_2)' happens at least once.

The requirement states that prop_0 should happen at least once, but only after both prop_1 and prop_2 have happened (the occurrence of prop_0 must not precede the occurrence of both prop_1 and prop_2). Hence, first, both prop_1 and prop_2 should happen (order between them is not specified), and after that, eventually, prop_0 occurs. Additionally, prop_0 should not occur before both prop_1 and prop_2 have occurred. This is captured by '(prop_0 -> (F prop_1 & F prop_2))' and '(¬prop_0 U (prop_1 & prop_2))', but to require that prop_0 actually occurs at some point after both, we use: '(F (prop_1 & prop_2 & F prop_0)) & (¬prop_0 U (prop_1 & prop_2))'.

The requirement states that the transition from prop_2 to prop_3 can only occur after both prop_0 and prop_1 have been true at some point. In other words, prop_3 cannot become true immediately following prop_2 unless prop_0 and prop_1 are both true at that time. So, whenever prop_2 holds and at the next time step prop_3 holds, then both prop_0 and prop_1 must be true at the present.

This specification requires that 'prop_0' must happen exactly once after both 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' have happened (occurred at least once each). In LTL, we use the 'eventually' operator to require that 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' both eventually occur, and after that, eventually, 'prop_0' will also occur. If you only want to force that 'prop_0' occurs after both have occurred, but not before, you can use the 'unless' or 'release' operator to block its occurrence before both 'prop_1' and 'prop_2'. Finally, to guarantee 'prop_0' occurs only once, we require that after its first true occurrence, it is never true again. This is achieved by requiring that, globally, once it happens, it is never true at any future point.

explanation: The requirement states that prop_2 shall occur once both prop_0 and prop_1 have occurred. In LTL, this is captured by stating: If prop_2 occurs, then in the past both prop_0 and prop_1 must have already occurred, and before both prop_0 and prop_1 have occurred, prop_2 should not occur. Thus, globally, until both prop_0 and prop_1 are true (either simultaneously or at different times), prop_2 does not occur. After that, at some point (eventually), prop_2 occurs.

The transition from Start-up Mode to Self test Mode occurs once both prop_0 and prop_1 are true. Interpreted in LTL, this means that eventually, both prop_0 and prop_1 will be true at the same time (i.e., there will exist a point in the future where prop_0 & prop_1 hold).

The statement requires that both prop_0 and prop_1 must occur at some point, and after both have occurred (possibly at the same time or one after the other in any order), prop_2 must occur once. We must enforce that prop_2 does not occur before both prop_0 and prop_1 have happened. After both have happened, eventually prop_2 must occur once (and only once, if strictly enforced).

This requirement states that after both prop_1 and prop_2 have occurred, prop_0 must occur (at least once) at some point afterward. In LTL, this is typically captured by saying it is always the case that if both prop_1 and prop_2 occur (either simultaneously or at any time in the past), then eventually prop_0 will occur.
